era_context: The Late Bronze Age, a period of interconnected Mediterranean and Near Eastern civilisations at their height. The New Kingdom of Egypt under Ramesses II dominates the eastern Mediterranean; the Hittite Empire controls Anatolia and northern Syria; Mycenaean Greece is in its palatial phase; the Shang Dynasty rules China; and complex societies flourish across the Indian subcontinent. This is an era of international diplomacy, trade networks, and military competition, though the entire system will collapse within a century.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa (limited archaeological record for this period)
Southeast Asia and Oceania (sparse written records)
Northern Europe (pre-literate societies, archaeological evidence only)

events.title: Ramesses II's reign — continued monumental construction and military consolidation
events.description: Ramesses II (r. 1279–1213 BCE) was in the prime of his reign in 1252 BCE, continuing his ambitious building programme and military campaigns. Major temples were under construction, including the Abu Simbel temples (begun c. 1264 BCE). His reign was characterised by unprecedented monumental output, military campaigns in Nubia and the Levant, and the consolidation of Egyptian imperial power. The pharaoh was at the height of his authority and prestige, with a large family and numerous offspring who would ensure dynastic continuity.

sources.name: Archaeological evidence from temple and palace sites

events.certainty_note: Ramesses II's reign is well-established through king lists, temple inscriptions, and synchronisms with other Near Eastern powers. His building programme is extensively documented.

events.title: Hittite Empire — military campaigns and diplomatic negotiations
events.description: Hattusili III was engaged in military campaigns and diplomatic negotiations in the 1250s BCE. The Hittite Empire faced threats from multiple directions: the Kassite kingdom of Babylon to the south, the Assyrian Empire to the east, and various Anatolian and Syrian rivals. Hattusili III's strategy combined military action with diplomacy, including the negotiation of the peace treaty with Egypt. The Hittite state was at the height of its power, though internal and external pressures would increase in subsequent decades.
events.certainty_note: Hattusili III's reign is well-documented in Hittite archives. His military and diplomatic activities are recorded in contemporary clay tablets.

events.title: Mycenaean Greece — palatial administration and Mediterranean trade
events.description: The Mycenaean palaces in the 1250s BCE were centres of economic and administrative activity. Linear B tablets record the management of agricultural production, textile manufacture, metalworking, and trade. The palaces controlled extensive territories and participated in Mediterranean trade networks, exchanging goods with Egypt, the Levant, Anatolia, and other regions. The palatial system was at its peak of complexity and power, though it would collapse within 50–75 years.

sources.name: Linear B tablets from Pylos, Mycenae, Thebes, and Khania
Archaeological evidence of palace workshops and storage facilities

events.certainty_note: Linear B tablets are contemporary administrative records. Archaeological stratigraphy places them in the Late Helladic IIIB phase (c. 1300–1200 BCE).

events.title: Shang Dynasty — bronze metallurgy and ritual vessel production
events.description: The Shang Dynasty was producing bronze vessels of exceptional quality and sophistication in the 1250s BCE. These vessels served ritual and ceremonial purposes, used in ancestor worship and state ceremonies. The bronze-working technology was highly advanced, with complex casting techniques and sophisticated designs. The vessels often bore inscriptions identifying the maker or the occasion for which they were made. Bronze weapons and tools were also produced, supporting both military and agricultural activities. The Shang state controlled the sources of tin and copper necessary for bronze production.
key_figures: Shang bronze-workers and artisans (names not recorded)

sources.name: Bronze vessels and weapons from archaeological excavations
Oracle bones recording divinations about bronze-working and ritual

events.certainty_note: Bronze artefacts are securely dated by archaeological stratigraphy. The sophistication of Shang bronze-working is evident in surviving vessels.

events.title: South Asian cultural transitions — post-Indus Valley period
events.region: Indus Valley and surrounding regions (modern Pakistan and northwest India)
events.description: The 1250s BCE saw continued cultural transitions in South Asia. The Indus Valley Civilisation had declined centuries earlier, but smaller settlements persisted, and new cultural patterns were emerging. Archaeological evidence suggests increasing influence from Indo-Aryan speaking peoples. The material culture shows a mixture of Indus Valley traditions and new elements. This period is poorly documented in written sources, and understanding of it relies primarily on archaeology and linguistic reconstruction.
events.certainty_note: The post-Indus Valley period is poorly understood due to sparse archaeological evidence and the absence of written records. Dating is based on stratigraphic and typological analysis.

disconfirming_evidence: No specific disconfirming evidence identified for events listed in 1252 BCE. The general chronology of the Late Bronze Age remains subject to scholarly debate, but the events themselves are not in dispute.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 1252 BCE is similar to that for 1250–1251 BCE: rich for the Near East and Mediterranean, sparse for other regions. Egyptian and Hittite sources are official court documents, likely to emphasise royal achievements. Linear B tablets are administrative records, not narrative histories. Oracle bones reveal Shang religious and administrative concerns but are fragmentary. The post-Indus Valley period is known primarily through archaeology, with significant gaps in understanding.

graph_edges.note: Ramesses II's building programme and military campaigns were concurrent.
Hattusili III's military and diplomatic activities were ongoing.
